Easy.title: Freedom of Speech v/s Public Order
Easy.context: A tech startup founder publishes a post on social media criticizing government policies. The government orders the removal of the post claiming it incites public disorder.

lawyer1.name: Advocate Sharma
lawyer1.argument: Your Honor, Article 19(1)(a) guarantees freedom of speech and expression to every citizen. The state cannot arbitrarily censor speech. The post is a legitimate criticism, not an incitement to violence.
points: Criticism of government is protected speech
No incitement to violence in the post
Censorship requires clear and present danger

lawyer2.name: Advocate Verma
lawyer2.argument: Your Honor, while freedom is important, Article 19(2) allows reasonable restrictions for public order. The post has already received 100,000 shares and is causing communal tensions. The state has the duty to maintain peace.
points: Article 19(2) allows restrictions for public order
Government has duty to protect citizens

choices.label: Founder Wins
choices.description: Speech is protected unless direct incitement

choices.label: Government Wins
choices.description: Post must be removed for public order

choices.label: Partial/Compromise
choices.description: Post removed but no legal action against founder

Easy.explanation: The Supreme Court has held that restrictions on Article 19 are valid if they are in the interest of public order and are reasonable. In this case, removing the post (which is causing tensions) is a reasonable restriction, but taking legal action against the founder would be excessive since the speech itself is political criticism, not direct incitement.

relevantArticles.title: Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression
Reasonable Restrictions on Freedom of Speech

Easy.title: Right to Life v/s Medical Ethics
Easy.context: A terminally ill patient with unbearable pain requests to end their life. The hospital refuses citing medical ethics and law against euthanasia.

lawyer1.name: Advocate Singh
lawyer1.argument: Your Honor, Article 21 protects the right to life, which includes the right to live with dignity. Forcing someone through unbearable suffering violates their dignity and personal autonomy.
points: Article 21 includes right to die with dignity

lawyer2.name: Advocate Kapoor
lawyer2.argument: Your Honor, while dignity is important, the right to life means protecting life itself. Doctors took oath to preserve life. Passive euthanasia with palliative care is the constitutional way.
points: Medical ethical duty to preserve life
Slippery slope risk of active euthanasia

choices.label: Allow Euthanasia
choices.description: Right to dignity includes right to choose death

choices.label: Disallow Euthanasia
choices.description: Only passive euthanasia and palliative care allowed

choices.label: Passive Euthanasia
choices.description: Allow withdrawal of life support with consent

Easy.explanation: The Indian Supreme Court in the Aruna Shanbaug case held that while active euthanasia is not allowed, passive euthanasia (withdrawing life support) is constitutionally valid when approved by medical board and family. This balances the right to life with the right to dignity.

relevantArticles.title: Protection of Life and Personal Liberty
Protection in Respect of Conviction for Offences

Easy.title: Equality v/s Reservations
Easy.context: A student from general category fails to get admission to medical school due to reservation of seats for SC/ST students, despite better marks.

lawyer1.name: Advocate Desai
lawyer1.argument: Your Honor, Article 14 guarantees equality before law. Denying admission based on caste, even with good marks, violates equality. Merit should be the only criterion.
points: Discrimination on either side is wrong

lawyer2.name: Advocate Rao
lawyer2.argument: Your Honor, equality also means removing historical inequalities. Articles 15 and 16 specifically allow affirmative action. Centuries of discrimination cannot be ignored.

choices.label: Reservations with Creamy Layer
choices.description: Reservations minus rich backward class families

Easy.explanation: The Supreme Court has consistently held that while reservations are constitutional under Articles 15 and 16 for social justice, they must exclude the creamy layer (economically well-off members from backward classes). This balances social justice with individual merit.

relevantArticles.title: Prohibition of Discrimination on Grounds of Religion, etc.
Equality of Opportunity in Matters of Public Employment

Easy.title: Religious Freedom v/s Uniform Civil Code
Easy.context: A Muslim woman seeks divorce through civil court procedures instead of following personal religious law. Religious leaders oppose this as interference in religious matters.

lawyer1.name: Advocate Das
lawyer1.argument: Your Honor, Articles 14 and 21 ensure equality. All citizens regardless of religion should have equal rights in marriage and divorce. The Constitution envisions a Uniform Civil Code.
points: All citizens should have same rights

lawyer2.name: Advocate Khan
lawyer2.argument: Your Honor, Articles 25 and 26 protect religious freedom. Personal laws of different religions are part of cultural identity. Not everyone wants a uniform code.

choices.label: Uniform Code Mandatory
choices.description: All must follow common civil law

choices.label: Choice Available
choices.description: Person can choose civil or religious law

Easy.explanation: Indian constitutional practice allows individuals to choose between personal laws and civil laws in many matters. The Supreme Court has held that while a Uniform Civil Code is aspirational, currently individuals retain the right to opt for their personal law or civil law procedures.

relevantArticles.title: Freedom of Conscience and Free Profession, Practice and Propagation of Religion

Easy.title: Right to Work v/s Child Labor Laws
Easy.context: A 13-year-old child from poor family works in a small textile factory to support family income. The factory owner argues the child needs to work for family survival.

lawyer1.name: Advocate Ghosh
lawyer1.argument: Your Honor, the article 39(f) says children from poor families deserve special help. The child is learning a skill and family needs income. Complete ban hardens their situation.

lawyer2.name: Advocate Mathew
lawyer2.argument: Your Honor, Article 24 absolutely prohibits child labor. No exception for poverty is permitted. Childhood is for education and development, not exploitation.
points: State has duty to provide alternative support

choices.label: Strict Prohibition
choices.description: No child labor under any circumstance

choices.label: Light Work + Education
choices.description: Part-time light work with mandatory schooling

Easy.explanation: Article 24 provides absolute prohibition on child labor in factories and hazardous industries. However, Article 39 directs state to provide free education. The constitutional solution is to compensate families through social security, education, and employment for parents, not to permit child labor.

relevantArticles.title: Prohibition of Employment of Children in Factories, Etc.
Directive Principles - Protection of Children and Youth
Right to Free and Compulsory Education

Medium.title: Right to Privacy v/s National Security
Medium.context: Government installs surveillance cameras and monitors all internet traffic claiming it's necessary to prevent terrorism. Privacy activists challenge this.

lawyer1.name: Advocate Saxena
lawyer1.argument: Your Honor, the Supreme Court in K.S. Puttaswamy case recognized privacy as fundamental right under Article 21. Blanket surveillance violates privacy even for national security.
points: Privacy is fundamental right under Article 21

lawyer2.name: Advocate Bhat
lawyer2.argument: Your Honor, national security overrides individual privacy temporarily. Terrorism is existential threat. Targeted, authorized surveillance with court approval is constitutional.

Medium.explanation: The Supreme Court in K.S. Puttaswamy case (2017) recognized privacy as fundamental right but allowed reasonable restrictions for public interest. Targeted surveillance with proper warrants, judicial oversight, and proportionality principle is constitutional.

relevantArticles.title: Protection of Life and Personal Liberty

Medium.title: Judicial Overreach v/s Social Justice
Medium.context: Supreme Court directs construction of missing children's homes across all states, allocating budgets and executing implementation, a role normally done by executive.

lawyer1.name: Advocate Pandit
lawyer1.argument: Your Honor, this violates separation of powers. Article 50 ensures independent judiciary but overseeing implementation is executive function. Courts should only adjudicate, not govern.

lawyer2.name: Advocate Mehra
lawyer2.argument: Your Honor, where executive fails to protect children's rights, courts must step in. Article 142 allows creative remedies. This is public interest litigation ensuring justice.
points: Article 142 empowers Supreme Court for justice

choices.label: Cease Judicial Overreach
choices.description: Court withdraws and lets executive act

Medium.explanation: The Supreme Court has evolved a balanced approach in cases like Vishal Jeet v. Union of India. Courts can monitor and direct but shouldn't directly implement/execute. They give directions and monitor compliance through follow-up hearings while executive implements.

Medium.title: Right to Occupy Land v/s Squatter Rights
Medium.context: Homeless families have occupied unused government land for 3 years without permission, building shanties. Government wants possession back; squatters claim right to livelihood.

lawyer1.name: Advocate Nair
lawyer1.argument: Your Honor, Article 21 guarantees right to livelihood and shelter. After occupying unused land for years, these families have right to continue. Society is not harmed.
points: Right to shelter is basic need

lawyer2.name: Advocate Iyer
lawyer2.argument: Your Honor, the land is government property. Permitting squatting sets dangerous precedent and encourages illegal occupation. Government can use land for public projects.

Medium.explanation: Courts have held that while government property rights cannot be ignored, eviction must be done humanely. Government must provide alternative accommodation or rehabilitation facilities, not just evict them to streets, to fulfill Article 21 obligations.

relevantArticles.title: Protection of Life and Personal Liberty

Medium.title: Free Speech v/s Hate Speech
Medium.context: A politician makes inflammatory speech against a religious community on social media, claiming political expression. Community members face violence.

lawyer1.name: Advocate Gupta
lawyer1.argument: Your Honor, political speech gets highest protection under Article 19(1)(a). People need to hear diverse views even if offensive. Prosecution chills democratic discourse.

lawyer2.name: Advocate Reddy
lawyer2.argument: Your Honor, speech that incites communal violence is not protected. This speech directly caused violence. Article 19(2) allows restrictions on speech that promotes hatred.
points: Rights of group must be protected

choices.label: Context-Based
choices.description: Restrict only speech directly causing violence

Medium.explanation: The Supreme Court in Dr. Ambedkar v. Maratha Mukti Sangram Samiti held that speech causing direct, incitement to violence can be restricted under Article 19(2). However, mere offensive or critical speech continues to enjoy protection.

relevantArticles.title: Promoting Enmity on Grounds of Religion

Medium.title: Minority Rights v/s Majority Rule
Medium.context: A state where 60% are Hindu wants to remove 40% Muslim minority language from school curriculum claiming 'national integration'. Minorities oppose this.

lawyer1.name: Advocate Kulkarni
lawyer1.argument: Your Honor, majority of state residents want national language. Maintaining minority language increases division. National integration requires common language.

lawyer2.name: Advocate Karim
lawyer2.argument: Your Honor, Article 30 protects minority right to conserve language and culture. India's strength is diversity. Majority cannot destroy minority identity.

choices.label: Optional Choice
choices.description: Offer minority language as optional subject

Medium.explanation: The constitution protects minority rights to education and language under Articles 29-30, but also recognizes state resources are limited. The balanced approach is to make minority language education optional or available in certain schools rather than mandatory for all.

relevantArticles.title: Right of Minorities to Establish Educational Institutions

Hard.title: Hindutva v/s Secular State
Hard.context: Government makes constitutional amendment to declare India as 'Hindu Nation' where Hindu values are supreme in state policy. Minorities challenge this.

lawyer1.name: Advocate Trivedi
lawyer1.argument: Your Honor, India's civilization is primarily Hindu. Acknowledging this civilizational foundation is not against minorities. India can be culturally Hindu while legally secular.
points: Can be culturally Hindu, legally secular

lawyer2.name: Advocate Chatterji
lawyer2.argument: Your Honor, Article 25 guarantees secular state. Declaring nation as Hindu violates Article 15, 16 (no discrimination). This violates basic structure doctrine.
points: Basic structure: secular state is immutable

Hard.explanation: In S.R. Bommai v. Union of India (1994), Supreme Court held that secularism is part of basic structure of the Constitution and cannot be amended. Article 368 cannot be used to change the secular character of Constitution. However, cultural recognition without legal discrimination might be permissible.

relevantArticles.title: Amendment of Constitution - Basic Structure Doctrine

Hard.title: Parliament Sovereignty v/s Judicial Review
Hard.context: Parliament passes law that court believes violates rights. Parliament claims its laws cannot be questioned by courts under Article 121. Citizen challenges law.

lawyer1.name: Advocate Dixit
lawyer1.argument: Your Honor, Article 121 forbids courts from questioning proceedings of Parliament. Democracy means people's representatives decide, not judges. Over-questioning makes courts superlegislatures.

lawyer2.name: Advocate Mishra
lawyer2.argument: Your Honor, Marbury v. Madison principle and Article 13 establish judicial review. Laws violating Constitution are ultra vires. Courts must protect constitutional rights.

choices.label: Balanced Review
choices.description: Review with deference to Parliament's judgment

Hard.explanation: The Constitution recognizes both parliamentary sovereignty and judicial review. Courts exercise 'reasonable review' - giving deference to Parliament while ensuring laws don't violate fundamental rights or basic structure. Article 121 prevents questioning of legislative process, not the constitutionality of laws.

relevantArticles.title: Laws Inconsistent with Constitution to be Void

Hard.title: Federalism v/s National Emergency
Hard.context: During national emergency, central government seizes state revenue, dismisses state elections, and suspends state autonomy claiming national security. States resist.

lawyer1.name: Advocate Agarwal
lawyer1.argument: Your Honor, Article 352 emergency powers are necessary for national survival. During emergency, center must have overwhelming power to manage crisis. State autonomy is secondary to national unity.

lawyer2.name: Advocate Rao
lawyer2.argument: Your Honor, federalism is basic structure. Even emergency cannot destroy it. Center can act but must respect state boundaries and restore normalcy quickly.

choices.label: Absolute Central Control
choices.description: Center has full power during emergency

choices.label: Limited Emergency Powers
choices.description: Center acts with safeguards and sunset clause

Hard.explanation: After the 1976 Emergency (Indira Gandhi), constitutional amendments added checks on emergency powers. Emergency is allowed but with prescribed duration, limitations, and parliamentary review. The 44th Amendment limited emergency to 6 months, establishing that even national emergency has constitutional limits.

relevantArticles.title: Failure of Constitutional Machinery in States

Hard.title: Corporate Rights v/s Public Interest
Hard.context: A pharmaceutical company's patent prevents production of cheap generic drugs for dying cancer patients. Company claims property and patent rights; patients claim right to life.

lawyer1.name: Advocate Kulshrestha
lawyer1.argument: Your Honor, patent is intellectual property protected under Articles 19(1)(g) and Article 300A. Patent holders invest billions in R&D. Compulsory licensing is economically unjust.

lawyer2.name: Advocate Sharma
lawyer2.argument: Your Honor, Article 21 (right to life) is more fundamental than patent rights. State can issue compulsory license for public health. Lives are more valuable than profit.
points: TRIPS allows exceptions for public health

choices.label: Compulsory License
choices.description: State issues license for public health

Hard.explanation: The Constitution and TRIPS Agreement allow compulsory licensing for public health emergency. The Bayer v. Natco case (Supreme Court of India) held that compulsory license for affordable generic production at reasonable royalty is constitutional, balancing patent rights with public health.

relevantArticles.title: State's Duty to Improve Public Health

Hard.title: Right to Protest v/s National Security
Hard.context: Environmental activists organize protests near defense installations against nuclear waste dumping. Government arrests them under AFSPA (Armed Forces Special Powers Act).

lawyer1.name: Advocate Bhattacharya
lawyer1.argument: Your Honor, Article 19(1)(a-c) guarantee rights to protest and assembly. AFSPA (1958) violates fundamental rights by giving military immunity and vague charges.

lawyer2.name: Advocate Singh
lawyer2.argument: Your Honor, defense installations need security. AFSPA areas face insurgency. National security cannot be compromised by protests. Reasonable restrictions under Article 19(3-4) apply.

choices.label: AFSPA with Safeguards
choices.description: AFSPA valid but with judicial oversight

Hard.explanation: In Naga People's Movement for Human Rights v. Union of India (1998), Supreme Court upheld AFSPA but imposed judicial oversight. The Court held that while AFSPA is valid, misuse can be challenged and immunity is not absolute. Officers must follow constitutional values.

relevantArticles.title: Power of Parliament to Modify Rights in Armed Forces
